trajectory_summary: This call moves from warm to booked on the back of a cooperative, returning solar shopper who did most of the heavy lifting herself. Randy executes the structural beats competently — verify, bill collection, scheduling, one-hour block — but leaves significant energy on the table by not anchoring Lauren's bill pain emotionally, not flipping her tree-removal enthusiasm into a stronger commitment signal, and not surfacing the co-decision-maker for appointment inclusion. The appointment is real but structurally thin: no co-decider confirmed, no emotional anchor built, and two unresolved financial questions heading into the closer call.
outcome_dispute: The appointment appears booked (Wednesday the 29th at 10:30, confirmed at 09:03), but it is conditionally fragile: the co-decision-maker on the title was identified at 02:13 but never included in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, an appointment booked without the co-decider present is structurally vulnerable. The v1 outcome tag of 'unknown' is consistent with the transcript ending — the bill photo had not yet been received at call close, which is a secondary fragility on a paid-lead type.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: There is. And anyone who makes the decisions.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Lauren names a co-decision-maker on the title and Randy books the appointment for Lauren alone. This is the single gap most likely to cost the sit — a closer who gets to proposal with only one title-holder present faces a structural stall. The fix is one sentence at scheduling: 'Let's make sure [name] is on the call with us — what time works for both of you?' Randy needs to hear protocols.spouse_decision not as a rule but as a paycheck-protection move: the appointment he booked is fragile until the co-decider is confirmed.

key_moments.quote: It is a single family home. We do have a couple of trees. We we do have some and I am happy to take them out or part of them or cut them way back
key_moments.stewart_read: Lauren is not just answering a question — she is proactively problem-solving a potential installation barrier before Randy raises it. A customer who volunteers to remove trees to make solar work is signaling strong commitment. Randy acknowledges it ('Sounds good. I can go ahead and put that down here for the notes') but treats it as a logistics note rather than an emotional anchor. The verify.interest_in_solar item is the right home for this signal — it is the deepest available window into Lauren's motivation, and Randy walks past it.

key_moments.quote: There is. And anyone who makes the decisions.
key_moments.stewart_read: Lauren confirms a co-decision-maker on the title and explicitly flags them as the decision-maker. The protocol is unambiguous: include the co-decider in the appointment. Randy's response ('Okay. Sounds good.') moves past this entirely. The violation is confirmed by the booking at 09:03 — the appointment is set for Lauren alone with no mention of the co-decider. Note: the line 'No worries. If I surprise it, we can go ahead' at 02:17 is spoken by Lauren (speaker_0), not Randy — Lauren is signaling she can proceed without the co-decider, which is exactly the dynamic the protocol is designed to prevent. Randy accepts Lauren's framing rather than redirecting to joint scheduling.

key_moments.quote: $100,145 a month year round. Okay. That that will go up.
key_moments.stewart_read: Lauren delivers the bill anchor and immediately adds the rising-cost signal — this is a two-part gift: current pain plus future pain trajectory. Randy's response ('Right. Okay. So it's going up. Gotcha. Okay. Awesome.') captures the data and moves to scheduling. The codex pattern is capture + flip into emotional alignment-against-utility. Randy executes capture only. The flip was available: Lauren's own words ('that will go up') are the setup — the rep just needed to reflect the pain back before pivoting.

key_moments.quote: It's I'm available between nine and eleven. Actually, between how long how long an appointment is this?
key_moments.stewart_read: Lauren pauses mid-scheduling to ask about appointment length — a time-constraint signal. Randy responds correctly: 'we usually block off about an hour for you just to ensure you don't feel rushed, but it really just depends on how many questions you have.' This is the one-hour block framing per button_up.one_hour_block_stated, delivered naturally and without defensiveness. Lauren immediately offers a specific window (10:30 to 11:30) and Randy confirms it. This is clean protocol execution.
